A ribbon was cut today… but the real win happens underground. ✂️💧 The new Kanasita Equalization Basin and Pump Station Project can store up to 5 million gallons of excess wastewater during heavy rain events, helping reduce sanitary sewer overflows and protect our neighborhoods, waterways, and community. And that’s not all—this project also replaced aging infrastructure and addressed long-standing odor concerns for nearby residents. Not every superhero wears a cape. Sometimes it’s a 5-million-gallon tank quietly doing its job beneath the surface. 😉 Today, we celebrate another major milestone for Clear Chattanooga and a cleaner, more resilient future for our city. 📸 Drone photo courtesy of Cool New Media. #ClearChattanooga #Chattanooga #Infrastructure #CommunityFirst #WastewaterWorks

We hear your concerns about the odors, and we want to show you exactly what’s happening behind the scenes at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. Upgrading an aging wastewater system is a massive, highly complex process. In the video below, you can get a firsthand look at the major infrastructure upgrades we are currently undertaking to protect our waterways and improve our community: 💡Massive Infrastructure: We manage over 1,200 miles of underground piping and 80 pump stations to process 65 million gallons of wastewater from the surrounding region every single day. 💡Preventing Overflows: To protect the Tennessee River during heavy rains, we are utilizing 30 million gallons of new storage capacity. In fact, the clean water we put back into the river is actually cleaner than the river itself! 💡Odor Control: We know the smell is an issue we need to tackle. We currently utilize five different odor control systems to manage the greenhouse gases emissions from daily sewage, as well as the 80 different local industries that discharge into our system.  💡Green Energy Innovation: We recently announced a new initiative to convert wastewater methane directly into electricity. This will eventually take our campus off the electric grid and save up to $300,000 a month in energy costs. These upgrades take time, but our staff is working hard every day to move our city’s systems forward. Want to better understand the science behind it all? Tap the link below or in our bio for a simple breakdown of exactly how our wastewater treatment process works:👇 https://www.local3news.com/…/article_13c310a8-d534-479f-97c…

We hear you, Chattanooga! 🗣️ It’s no secret that facility upgrades have been a long time coming, and our team is hard at work making them a reality. We are proud to be working alongside Mayor Tim Kelly to implement these crucial improvements at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. Thank you to our community for your patience as we build a cleaner, fresher future for our city! Read more about the timeline and what’s underway below: 👇💧WHAT'S THAT SMELL? For years, Chattanooga residents have occasionally noticed unpleasant sewage odors drifting across parts of the city. Mayor Tim Kelly says significant improvements underway at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus should help reduce those smells in the coming years.⬇️

Wrapping up an incredible final day at the Tennessee Environmental Network Show of the South Conference! 🌿 A massive thank you to our speakers — Bill Cannon, Jacob McCrary, Travis Jones, and David Taylor — for representing the evolution happening within the Wastewater Department. We aren’t just a “sewer plant” anymore. We are stepping into our true identity as the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. We’re shifting from stigma to stewardship, from waste to raw materials, and from consumption to circularity. That shift is exactly why we took home the 2025 Environmental Stewardship Award for Water Quality! 🏆 But we’re equally proud of what’s happening internally. From crushing 6,000+ work orders a year to launching our new employee-focused “Operation Career Ladder” this July, we are turning everyday labor into lifelong, empowered careers. Protecting our environment, empowering our people. Water to water. Earth to earth. 🌍💧

Exciting things happening in Chatt 🔥🔥We’re here to answer the question you’ve been asking — what are those gigantic structures popping up along I-75? In a way to combat sanitary sewer overflows, our Wastewater Department has been hard at work with its partners to construct e2i2 Equalization Stations. During a storm event, these will: Send wastewater from the sewer lines to the diversion structure, that then funnels to a 48-inch gravity line (that’s about 20-ft deep) toward the pump storage. The pump storage will then send it to one of the two 15 million-gallon tanks (made of steel and shotcrete). This process will be powered by an electrical house with an 800kw generator and there will also be odor control ventilation for the pump station and tanks. Once the storm event is over, our Wastewater team will send the water downstream through the gravity line that connects to the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus where it will get treated before being sent back to the Tennessee River. Construction began in 2025 and is on track for its 2027 completion. So far, the project has also generated $39 million in local investment and supporting jobs in the region.

We are proud to introduce the new official logo for the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus! 🛡️ As our facilities and technologies have evolved, we wanted a logo that reflects the true scope of our work today. Our new shield highlights the four pillars of our modern environmental mission: ⚡ Energy: Harnessing power and resource recovery. ♻️ Sustainability: Commitment to recycling and protecting our local ecosystem. 🌊 Clean Water: Returning safe, clean water back to the Tennessee River. ⚙️ Science & Engineering: Using innovation to drive our operations forward. We are so much more than what goes down the drain; we are proud stewards of Chattanooga’s environment. Let us know what you think of the new look in the comments! 👇

As we celebrate Women's History Month, I want to thank the women who work for the City of Chattanooga. These are just some of the brilliant minds and warm faces I see around City Hall, but you'll see hundreds of more across our departments — from first responders to the GIS team and those advancing our economic and community development opportunities.
